questions.question: What is the main reason uptime alone is usually a weak primary SLI?
options: It can stay healthy while users still see slow or failed important actions.
It is impossible to collect uptime data in distributed systems.
It automatically counts all latency as success regardless of thresholds.
It only works for internal platforms and never for public APIs.
questions.explanation: A host or service can be technically up while the user experience is still poor.

questions.question: What does an error budget represent?
options: The maximum number of dashboards a team may create before launch freeze.
The fraction of unreliability allowed by the SLO before the target is violated.
The total number of alerts allowed in a quarter across all services.
The amount of logging storage reserved for failed requests.
questions.explanation: Error budget is the allowed bad-event share implied by the SLO target.

questions.question: Which SLI is usually stronger for a user-facing read API?
options: The percentage of user requests completed successfully under a meaningful latency threshold.
The percentage of hosts that responded to a health probe in the last hour.
The number of engineers currently assigned to the service.
The total count of log lines produced by the API fleet.
questions.explanation: User-meaningful success and latency better reflect experience than infrastructure-only health.

questions.question: Why might a team split read and write SLOs instead of using one blended objective?
options: Because read and write paths often have different user value, traffic shape, and failure characteristics.
Because blended objectives are not allowed in multi-service systems.
Because write traffic can never be measured reliably.
Because separate SLOs eliminate the need for alerts.
questions.explanation: Distinct paths often deserve separate targets to avoid hiding meaningful pain.

questions.question: What does a high burn rate over a short window usually tell you?
options: The service is consuming error budget faster than the elapsed time would justify.
The metrics backend has too much storage available.
The request volume is lower than expected but still healthy.
The team should always tighten the SLO target immediately.
questions.explanation: Burn rate compares budget consumption speed to time elapsed in the window.

questions.question: Which trade-off comes with setting an extremely tight SLO target?
options: It may drive higher cost and reduce room for launches or operational mistakes.
It guarantees zero incidents without additional engineering work.
It removes the need to define an SLI carefully.
It makes latency distributions less important.
questions.explanation: Stricter objectives shrink the budget and usually demand more operational investment.

questions.question: What is a common sign that an SLI is poorly chosen?
options: It stays green during incidents that clearly affect users or support channels.
It is based on completed requests rather than host health.
It has an explicit numerator and denominator.
It uses a rolling time window.
questions.explanation: A green metric during obvious user pain usually means the SLI is not measuring the right thing.

questions.question: What is the strongest senior-level answer to 'What do you do when the error budget burns too fast?'
options: Adjust paging, rollout, or reliability priorities based on the burn rather than treating the SLO as a vanity report.
Ignore it because traffic bursts are expected in distributed systems.
Delete the SLO and rely only on infrastructure alarms.
Lower the denominator until the SLI looks healthier.
questions.explanation: The point of error budget is to drive operational and product decisions.
